message.content: Adversaries may compromise numerous third-party systems to form a botnet, a network of compromised systems that can be instructed to perform coordinated tasks. Instead of purchasing or renting a botnet, adversaries may build their own by compromising numerous systems or taking over an existing botnet by redirecting bots to adversary-controlled command and control (C2) servers. Botnets can be used for various malicious activities, including large-scale phishing campaigns and Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks. Notable examples include Axiom using compromised machines as proxy nodes and the Sandworm Team targeting Small Office/Home Office (SOHO) network devices with a large-scale botnet. Mitigation strategies include implementing robust network security measures, such as firewalls and intrusion detection systems, regularly updating and patching systems, and employing network segmentation to limit the spread of botnet infections. Additionally, monitoring for unusual network traffic patterns can help in early detection and response to botnet activities.
